titleEn: A simpler way to monitor manure temperature in deep-pit swine barns
titleZh: 深坑猪舍粪污温度监测新方法：一个传感器就够了
summaryZh: 爱荷华州立大学一项为期一年的研究发现，在深坑猪舍中，仅需一个温度传感器即可准确监测粪污温度（误差±2°C以内），从而有效评估微生物活动、气体排放（尤其是甲烷）和舍内空气质量。该研究通过高分辨率数据统计验证，推翻了“传感器越多越好”的传统认知，为养殖场提供了低成本、高可靠性的监测方案，对智慧畜牧中的精准环境管理和减排实践具有重要参考价值。
translationZh:
深坑猪舍中的粪污温度对许多养殖户而言，其重要性可能远超想象。它直接影响微生物活动、气体排放（包括甲烷）以及整体舍内空气质量。由于温度驱动这些过程，准确测量粪污温度对于了解舍内状况、估算排放量以及做出明智的管理决策至关重要。然而，目前大多数方法依赖有限的数据或对全年粪污温度的笼统假设。在许多情况下，养殖户和研究人员在估算甲烷排放时仅使用年度平均值。虽然方便，但这种方法会引入显著的不确定性，因为实际粪污温度随季节、深度和坑内位置而变化。

**相关阅读：** 韧性育成舍：十年表现与经济波动

为弥补这一空白，爱荷华州立大学的研究人员开展了一项为期一年的研究，旨在更深入地了解深坑育肥舍内粪污温度的变化规律——更重要的是，确定实际需要多少个温度传感器才能准确监测。

**测量坑内真实情况**

该研究在爱荷华州中部的两栋商业化深坑育肥舍中进行——一栋配备坑内通风，另一栋则没有。研究人员在每个猪舍内的不同位置以及粪污的三个不同深度安装了多个温度传感器。这些传感器在近一整年内每小时收集一次数据，形成了一个详细的数据集，捕捉了季节性变化和坑内差异。

这一高分辨率数据集使团队能够评估粪污温度在整个猪舍内以及随时间的变化情况。同时，它也提供了一个基准，用于测试使用更少传感器能否代表整体温度。

**寻找最少传感器数量**

研究人员并未假设传感器越多数据越好，而是采用统计方法，将每一种可能的传感器组合与根据全部传感器计算出的“真实”平均温度进行比较。他们设定了一个实用阈值：如果一个传感器（或一组传感器）能够将平均粪污温度的估算值控制在完整数据集±2°C以内，则认为其精度足以满足实际应用。关键问题很简单：到底需要多少个传感器才能获得可靠测量？

**令人惊讶的结果：一个传感器就足够了。** 结果清晰且可能令人惊讶。在两栋猪舍以及整个监测期间，所有
featuredReason: 该研究以严谨的统计方法颠覆了“多传感器才可靠”的直觉，为智慧畜牧中的低成本精准监测提供了直接可落地的方案，尤其对猪场甲烷减排和环境管理具有重要实践意义。
contentFull: Farming Business ManagementManureFarm FacilitiesFarming EquipmentResearchA simpler way to monitor manure temperature in deep-pit swine barnsHow many sensors do you actually need to get a reliable measurement?Iowa State UniversityJune 19, 2026schedule4 Min ReadNational Pork BoardBy Wyatt Kendall, Brett Ramirez, Hanwook Chung, and Daniel Andersen; Agricultural and Biosystems Engineering, Iowa State UniversityManure temperature in deep-pit swine barns plays a much bigger role than many producers may realize. It directly affects microbial activity, gas emissions—including methane—and overall barn air quality. Because temperature drives these processes, getting an accurate measure of manure temperature is important for understanding barn conditions, estimating emissions and making sound management decisions. However, most current approaches rely on limited data or generalized assumptions about manure temperature throughout the year. In many cases, producers and researchers simply use an annual average value when estimating methane emissions. While convenient, this approach can introduce significant uncertainty because real manure temperatures vary by season, depth and location within the pit. Related:The resilient nursery: A decade of performance and economic volatilityTo address this gap, researchers at Iowa State University conducted a year-long study to better understand how manure temperature behaves inside deep-pit finishing barns—and, importantly, to determine how many temperature sensors are actually needed to monitor it accurately.Measuring what’s really happening in the pitThe study was carried out in two commercial deep-pit finishing barns in central Iowa—one with pit ventilation and one without. Researchers installed multiple temperature sensors at different locations within each barn, and at three different depths within the manure. These sensors collected data every hour for nearly a full year, creating a detailed dataset that captured both seasonal changes and differences within the pit.This high-resolution dataset allowed the team to evaluate how manure temperature varies throughout the barn and over time. It also provided a benchmark for testing how well smaller numbers of sensors could represent the overall temperature.Finding the minimum number of sensorsRather than assuming more sensors always lead to better data, the researchers used a statistical approach to compare every possible combination of sensors against the “true” average temperature calculated from the full set of sensors. They set a practical threshold: if a sensor (or group of sensors) could estimate the average manure temperature within ±2°C of the full dataset, it would be considered accurate enough for real-world use. The key question was simple: How many sensors do you actually need to get a reliable measurement?The surprising result: one sensor is enough. The results were clear—and likely surprising. Across both barns and throughout the entire monitoring period, all sensor configurations were statistically equivalent within the 2°C threshold. In practical terms, this means that manure temperature was largely uniform across the deep pit.Related:Decade of decline: EU pig population down nearly 9%Because of this uniformity, a single properly placed sensor—installed at an appropriate depth and location—was sufficient to represent the average temperature of the entire manure pit. While some variation did occur, especially during seasonal transitions or after manure removal events, these differences were small and did not significantly impact the overall average temperature.Why this matters for producersThis finding has important practical implications for swine producers, integrators and researchers.Lower equipment costs. Installing multiple sensors throughout a barn can be expensive and time-consuming. This study shows that one well-placed sensor can provide reliable temperature data, reducing hardware and installation costs.Simpler data management. Fewer sensors mean less data to collect, transmit and analyze. This simplifies monitoring systems and reduces the burden on farm staff or technical teams.Improved confidence in emission estimates. Methane emissions from manure storage are heavily influenced by temperature. By using accurate temperature data—even from a single sensor—producers and researchers can improve the reliability of emission calculations and environmental reporting.Better-informed management decisions. Accurate temperature monitoring can support decisions related to manure handling, storage and ventilation strategies, as well as help evaluate changes in barn design or management practices.Related:Alltech releases 2025 Sustainability ReportA practical takeawayFor most non-partitioned, deep-pit swine barns, manure temperature appears to be more uniform than previously assumed. This means producers do not need a complex network of sensors to monitor it effectively.Instead, the focus should be on proper placement and maintenance of a single sensor—ensuring it is submerged at the right depth and functioning correctly. When done properly, this approach can provide the data needed to support both day-to-day management and long-term environmental goals.Looking aheadThis research provides a practical framework for improving how manure temperature is monitored in swine production systems. By combining detailed field measurements with statistical analysis, it offers a clearer, more cost-effective path forward.Ultimately, simplifying sensor strategies without sacrificing accuracy helps producers adopt precision livestock technologies that are both practical and affordable—supporting better environmental stewardship and more efficient production systems.About the AuthorIowa State contentHtml: How many sensors do you actually need to get a reliable measurement?
